title: Specific heat capacity
short: The heat needed to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1 K, measured in J/(kg·K).
full:
Specific heat capacity c tells you how much heat a material soaks up per kilogram for each degree of temperature rise: Q = c·m·ΔT. Materials with a high specific heat (water, c ≈ 4186 J/(kg·K)) warm up and cool down slowly, which is why water is used as a coolant and why coastal climates are mild.

Metals have low specific heats, so a small amount of heat changes their temperature a lot.

The SI unit is the joule per kilogram-kelvin, J/(kg·K).
